nodes.title: Antivirus software
nodes.dateDetails: Many competing claims to the first true antivirus software product, but virtually all claims are from 1987. There were viruses and programs to remove them starting in 1971.
nodes.details: From Wikipedia: "There are competing claims for the innovator of the first antivirus product. Possibly, the first publicly documented removal of an "in the wild" computer virus (the "Vienna virus") was performed by Bernd Fix in 1987. In 1987, Andreas Lüning and Kai Figge, who founded G Data Software in 1985, released their first antivirus product for the Atari ST platform. In 1987, the Ultimate Virus Killer (UVK) was also released. This was the de facto industry standard virus killer for the Atari ST and Atari Falcon, the last version of which (version 9.0) was released in April 2004. In 1987, in the United States, John McAfee founded the McAfee company and, at the end of that year, he released the first version of VirusScan. Also in 1987 (in Czechoslovakia), Peter Paško, Rudolf Hrubý, and Miroslav Trnka created the first version of NOD antivirus."

nodes.dateDetails: "in the 1630s"

nodes.title: Banjo
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate, based on the earliest text description (by Richard Jobson in The Gambia) of what might be a banjo. Likely developed over time in the Americas from earlier African instruments before achieving its modern form in the 19th century.

nodes.title: Pleasure wheel
nodes.dateDetails: May have originated in 17th-centiury Ottoman Bulgaria. Described in "The Travels of Peter Mundy in Europe and Asia, 1608–1667" in Plovdiv

nodes.title: Keplerian refracting telescope
organizations: Court of the Holy Roman Empire

nodes.title: Ship of the line
nodes.subtitle: Prince Royal
nodes.dateDetails: Gradual evolution from carracks; the Prince Royal built in 1610 is a reasonable candidate for the first true ship of the line, and clearly the first ship of the line with three complete gun decks.

nodes.title: Kepler's laws of planetary motion
organizations: Court of the Holy Roman Empire

nodes.title: Thermoscope
nodes.dateDetails: Date uncertain, and attribution shared by several people. Inspired by ancient experiments, such as that of Philo of Byzantium

nodes.title: Bayonet
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest recorded publication, in the Chinese military treatise Binglu

nodes.title: Oxygen (Sendigovius)
nodes.details: very likely discovered by Sendigovius in 1604, but then re-discovered in the 1770s, from which modern oxygen chemistry derives

nodes.title: Pendulum physics
nodes.dateDetails: First experimental report by Galileo

nodes.title: Trompe and Catalan forge
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate

nodes.title: Xun lei chong
nodes.details: a revolving-barrel, spear-combined musket

nodes.title: Revolver
nodes.details: There were earlier designs of revolving firearms, e.g. c. 1580, but Stopler's design is often called the first revolver

nodes.title: Slitting mill
nodes.dateDetails: Known to have appeared in England from Belgium in 1590

nodes.title: Tjasker
nodes.dateDetails: "The tjasker seems to have been developed somewhere towards the end of the 16th century, though there is no conclusive date for its invention." There is a depiction from 1589 (shown in the image)

nodes.title: Sector
nodes.subtitle: aka Sector rule, Proportional compass, Military compass

nodes.title: Miquelet lock
nodes.dateDetails: Mentioned in the historical novel "Civil Wars of Granada", set in 1567-1571

nodes.title: Graphite pencil
nodes.dateDetails: Date of the discovery of the Borrowdale graphite deposit in England, which enabled the use of rods of graphite for writing and drawing, among other uses

nodes.title: Double bass
nodes.dateDetails: Very approximate

nodes.title: Condom
nodes.dateDetails: "In 16th-century Italy, Gabriele Falloppio authored the earliest uncontested description of condom use. De Morbo Gallico ("The French Disease", referring to syphilis) was published in 1564, two years after Falloppio's death."

nodes.title: Flintlock musket
nodes.dateDetails: Associated with the snaphance. Later, in the 17th century, muskets used the "true" flintlock mechanism

nodes.title: Snaphance
nodes.dateDetails: Late 1550s. "In Swedish the word Snapphane is first recorded 1558".

nodes.title: Hydrochloric acid
nodes.subtitle: aka Muriatic acid, Spirits of salt
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest recipes for the production of hydrochloric acid are found in Giovanni Battista Della Porta's Magiae naturalis ("Natural Magic")". Magiae naturalis was first published in Naples in 1558.

nodes.title: Baroque guitar
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest attestation of a five-stringed guitar comes from the mid-sixteenth-century Spanish book Declaracion de Instrumentos Musicales by Juan Bermudo, published in 1555."

nodes.title: Dulcian
nodes.dateDetails: probably earlier, bur early evidence is scant

nodes.title: Variolation
nodes.dateDetails: The first clear reference to smallpox inoculation was made by the Chinese author Wan Quan (1499–1582) in his Douzhen xinfa (痘疹心法) published in 1549.

nodes.title: Snaplock
nodes.dateDetails: "In 1547 a corresponding law in Florence in describing the different locks speak of matchlock, wheellock and a lock with stone and steel. In this cases it is more or less inarguable that the weapons in question are snaplocks. Arsenal accounts from 1547 in Sweden also explicitly mention snaplocks, and continued to do so in 1548 and beyond." May date from 1517, but this is less certain.

nodes.title: Ether
nodes.subtitle: aka Diethyl ether
nodes.dateDetails: "The compound may have been synthesised by either Jābir ibn Hayyān in the 8th century or Ramon Llull in 1275," but Valerius Cordus's 1540 synthesis seems more definite. This is also approximately the date of Paracelsus discovering its analgesic properties.

nodes.title: Galleon
nodes.subtitle: São João Baptista
nodes.dateDetails: Building date of the famous São João Baptista galleon in Portugal. There is no clear cutoff as to what counts as a "galleon".

nodes.title: Violin, viola, and cello
nodes.dateDetails: approximate

nodes.title: Laudanum
nodes.details: Paracelsus popularized opium in Europe and coined the word laudanum, but his opium pill differed significantly from Sydenham's tincture of opium in the 1660s, which became standard until the 20th century

nodes.title: Rifling
nodes.dateDetails: either Gaspard Kollner in 1498 or Augustus Kotter in 1520

nodes.title: Wheellock
nodes.details: Sometimes attributed to Leonardo da Vinci, in a drawing from the 1490s or the 1500s, but may also be from an anonymous German mechanic.

nodes.title: Tin-mercury amalgam mirror
nodes.dateDetails: date and precise location unknown, but "by the 16th century Venice was a center of mirror production using this technique"

nodes.title: Guillotine
nodes.subtitle: Halifax Gibbet
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate. It is not known when the Halifax Gibbet was introduced, but it was probably in the 16th century.

nodes.title: Long division
nodes.subtitle: Danda method
nodes.dateDetails: First printed long division algorithm, by Caldrini. The modern algorithm was devised c. 1600 by Briggs

nodes.title: Trombone
nodes.subtitle: aka Sackbut
nodes.dateDetails: first evidence, in a fresco by Filippino Lippi (shown as the image)

nodes.title: Viola da gamba
nodes.subtitle: aka Viol
nodes.dateDetails: mid-to-late 15th century

nodes.title: Arquebus and matchlock
nodes.dateDetails: The exact dating of the matchlock's appearance is disputed. It could have appeared in the Ottoman Empire as early as 1465 and in Europe a little before 1475.

nodes.title: Coffee
nodes.dateDetails: The discovery of the plant is attributed to legendary goatherd Kaldi circa 850 in Ethiopia, but "the earliest credible evidence of coffee drinking or knowledge of the coffee tree appears in the middle of the 15th century in the accounts of Ahmed al-Ghaffar in Yemen"

nodes.title: Rain gauge
nodes.dateDetails: First standardized rain gauge, though there were earlier rain measurements in Greece, India, China, etc.

nodes.title: Printing press
nodes.countryHistorical: Holy Roman Empire, Electorate of Mainz
nodes.formattedLocation: Holy Roman Empire; Electorate of Mainz

nodes.title: Mainspring
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest existing spring driven clock is the chamber clock given to Phillip the Good, Duke of Burgundy, around 1430"

nodes.title: Seaweed farming
nodes.dateDetails: Cultivation of gim is mentioned in a 1425 Korean book

nodes.title: Full plate armour
nodes.dateDetails: "By about 1420, complete suits of plate armour had been developed in Europe."

nodes.title: Clavichord
nodes.dateDetails: earliest mention, in a German poem; probably dates from the early 14th century

nodes.title: Carrack
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate

nodes.title: Paper cartridge
nodes.dateDetails: "Paper cartridges have been in use for nearly as long as hand-held firearms, with a number of sources dating their use back to the late 14th century."

nodes.title: Balance wheel
nodes.dateDetails: earliest existing drawing of a balance wheel, in Giovanni de Dondi's astronomical clock, built 1364, Padua, Italy

nodes.title: Naval mine
nodes.details: Naval mines were first invented by Chinese innovators of Imperial China and were described in thorough detail by the early Ming dynasty artillery officer Jiao Yu, in his 14th-century military treatise known as the Huolongjing.

nodes.title: Multistage rocket
nodes.subtitle: Huolongchushui
nodes.details: An illustration and description in the 14th century Chinese Huolongjing by Jiao Yu and Liu Bowen shows the oldest known multistage rocket; this was the "fire-dragon issuing from the water" (火龙出水, huǒ lóng chū shuǐ), which was used mostly by the Chinese navy.

nodes.title: Fragmentation bomb
nodes.dateDetails: Described in the Huolongjing, dated 1360-1375

nodes.title: Volley gun
nodes.subtitle: Ribauldequin or organ gun
nodes.dateDetails: "The first known ribauldequin was used by the army of Edward III of England in 1339 in France during the Hundred Years' War."

nodes.title: Hourglass
nodes.dateDetails: unclear origin; first clear evidence on a 1338 painting. Possibly found on a sarcophagus dated c. 350

nodes.title: Worm drive roller gin
nodes.countryHistorical: Yadava dynasty, Vijayanagara Empire, Bahmani Sultanate
nodes.formattedLocation: Yadava dynasty; Vijayanagara Empire; Bahmani Sultanate
nodes.details: 13th or 14th century: Worm gear cotton gin in Peninsular India (i.e. probably under the Yadava dynasty although may also be the Vijayanagara Empire or Bahmani Sultanate).

nodes.title: Zinc smelting
nodes.dateDetails: Recognized as a metal in the Rasaratna Samuchaya, an Indian alchemical treatise from the 13th-16th centuries

nodes.title: Dry compass
nodes.dateDetails: traditionally attributed to Flavio Gioja c. 1300, though he may not have existed

nodes.title: Jacob's staff
nodes.dateDetails: First described by Gersonides in 14th century Provence; may have been invented by Jacob ben Makir, who died in 1304

nodes.title: Nitric acid
nodes.subtitle: aka Aqua fortis
nodes.dateDetails: "The conventional view is that nitric acid was first described in pseudo-Geber's De inventione veritatis ("On the Discovery of Truth", after c. 1300)." May have been described earlier by Arab writers

nodes.title: Iceland spar
nodes.dateDetails: Speculated to be the "sunstone" used for navigation by medieval Norse sailors

nodes.title: Ice skates with sharp edge
nodes.dateDetails: "The Dutch added edges to ice skates in the 13th or 14th century."

nodes.title: Caravel
nodes.dateDetails: First documented use of the word "caravela" in the Portuguese language

nodes.title: Bagpipes
nodes.dateDetails: May be more ancient, but first certain evidence dates from mid-13th century Iberia

nodes.title: Rocket
nodes.dateDetails: May have been earlier, but first mention is a Song navy in a military exercise dated to 1245.

nodes.title: Cannon
nodes.subtitle: Wuwei Bronze Cannon
nodes.dateDetails: approximate date of the Wuwei Bronze Cannon, Xia dynasty
nodes.details: "The earliest known depiction of a cannon is a sculpture from the Dazu Rock Carvings in Sichuan dated to 1128, however, the earliest archaeological samples and textual accounts do not appear until the 13th century. The primary extant specimens of cannon from the 13th century are the Wuwei Bronze Cannon dated to 1227, the Heilongjiang hand cannon dated to 1288, and the Xanadu Gun dated to 1298. However, only the Xanadu gun contains an inscription bearing a date of production, so it is considered the earliest confirmed extant cannon." The image shows the Heilongjiang hand cannon.

nodes.title: Junk rig
nodes.subtitle: Chinese lugsail
nodes.dateDetails: Oldest depiction from Bayon temple, Cambodia, late 12th or 13th century.
nodes.details: Not Chinese in origin, although typically associated with Chinese junks

nodes.title: Eruptor
nodes.dateDetails: late 12th or early 13th century
nodes.details: Intermediate form between fire lances and cannons, when fire lances began being made of metal and put on frames

nodes.title: Counterweight trebuchet
nodes.dateDetails: first mention, but it's probably older

nodes.title: Post windmill
nodes.dateDetails: first windmills in Europe appear in the 12th century; first reference in 1185 in Yorkshire

nodes.title: Domestication of vanilla
nodes.dateDetails: "The Totonac people, who live along the eastern coast of Mexico in the present-day state of Veracruz, were among the first people to domesticate vanilla, cultivated on farms since at least 1185."

nodes.title: Shawm
nodes.dateDetails: Medieval European version of double-reed instruments from the Eastern Mediterranean, like the aulos and zurna. Came to Europe during the time of the crusades (12th century)

nodes.title: Sulfuric acid
nodes.subtitle: aka Oil of vitriol
nodes.dateDetails: Difficult to date. Using 1100 with this justification: "According to Ahmad Y. al-Hassan, three recipes for sulfuric acid occur in an anonymous Karshuni manuscript containing a compilation taken from several authors and dating from before c. 1100 AD".

nodes.title: Calico
nodes.dateDetails: 11th century

nodes.title: Buisine
nodes.subtitle: aka Añafil
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate; the name buisine refers to European medieval trumpets that developed from trumpets found during the Crusades or in Muslim Iberia, but it may refer to several distinct instruments. Commonly considered to be the ancestor of modern brass instruments.

nodes.title: Broad sheet glass
nodes.dateDetails: Theophilus's De Diversis Artibus (c. 1100–1120) describes the technique in practical detail; the Augsburg Cathedral prophet windows (late 11th century) are the earliest surviving examples

nodes.title: Coke (fuel)
nodes.dateDetails: May need to be adjusted. Based on this from Wikipedia: "By the first decades of the 11th century, Chinese ironworkers in the Yellow River valley began to fuel their furnaces with coke, solving their fuel problem in that tree-sparse region. By 1078 CE, the implementation of coke as a replacement to charcoal in the production of iron in China dramatically increased the industry to 125,000 tons per year."

nodes.title: Steelmaking with partial decarbonization
nodes.dateDetails: Described by Shen Kuo in 1075
nodes.details: Medieval process akin to the modern Bessemer process

nodes.title: Dry dock
nodes.dateDetails: Based on Song dynasty writing Shen Kuo. A dry dock was possibly used in antiquity around 200 AD for the Tessarakonteres ship, but this is highly uncertain.

nodes.title: Compass
nodes.dateDetails: first reference to the compass as a navigational instrument, in a Song dynasty book dated c. 1040-1044. Reached other regions like Europe and the Middle East in the 1100s-1200s

nodes.title: Paper recycling
nodes.dateDetails: "Paper recycling was first recorded in 1031 when Japanese shops sold repulped paper."

nodes.title: Spinning wheel
nodes.dateDetails: Disputed, could be much older in China or India; but the earliest clear evidence is the 11th-century Islamic world

nodes.title: Paper money
nodes.subtitle: Jiaozi
nodes.details: Jiaozi: considered the first true paper money

nodes.title: Organistrum
nodes.dateDetails: "end of the 10th or beginning of the 11th century"

nodes.title: Fulling mill
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate. There may evidence of a fulling mill in Roman Syria in 73-74

nodes.title: Fuse
nodes.subtitle: aka Fuze
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate. 10th century Song China

nodes.title: Tofu
nodes.dateDetails: First appearance in the written record. May be much earlier, and popularly attributed to Han dynasty prince Liu An (164 BC-122 BC)

nodes.title: Fountain pen
nodes.dateDetails: Supposedly commissioned by Fatimid caliph al Mu'izz li-Din Allah. There were many developments toward modern fountain pens in 17th and 18th century Europe.

nodes.title: Vielle
nodes.subtitle: aka Medieval fiddle
nodes.dateDetails: Hard to date precisely. The image, dated 900-950, might be the earliest European representation of something that can reasonably be called a vielle

nodes.title: Castle
nodes.subtitle: Château de Doué-la-Fontaine
nodes.details: image shows the Castle Site of Montbazon, built in the late 10th or 11th century

nodes.title: Fierce-fire oil cabinet
nodes.subtitle: aka Pen Huo Qi
nodes.details: Double-piston pump naphtha flamethrower; Chinese version of Greek fire, plausibly acquired indirectly (through Arabia) from the West

nodes.title: Byzantine lyra
nodes.dateDetails: approximate; first known depiction from a casket dated 900-1100 AD; also discussed by a Persian writer who died in 911

nodes.title: Psaltery
nodes.dateDetails: Evidence from the 9th century. The psaltery is the European evolution of the zither

nodes.title: Frequency analysis
nodes.dateDetails: approximate, based on al-Kindi's lifetime

nodes.title: Ethanol (isolation)
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate, based on the lifetime of Arab alchemists: "The distillation of wine is attested in Arabic works attributed to al-Kindī (c. 801–873 CE) and to al-Fārābī (c. 872–950), and in the 28th book of al-Zahrāwī's (Latin: Abulcasis, 936–1013) Kitāb al-Taṣrīf"

nodes.title: Dhow
nodes.dateDetails: date of the Belitung shipwreck, the oldest material evidence of a dhow. Likely older.

nodes.title: Sine quadrant
nodes.dateDetails: Al-Khwarizmi worked at the House of Wisdom around that time

nodes.title: Flying cash
nodes.details: Precursor to true banknotes during Tang dynasty

nodes.title: Panemone windmill
nodes.dateDetails: at least by the 9th century and possibly as early as the mid-to-late 7th century
nodes.details: first practical windmill

nodes.title: Rebab
nodes.dateDetails: no later than the 8th century

nodes.title: Oud
nodes.dateDetails: Very approximate. Emerged as an improvement of earlier instruments during the Islamic Golden Age

nodes.title: Xylophone
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest evidence in 9th-century Southeast Asia. Evolved (likely) independently in Africa at an unspecified ancient date, from where it spread to North America to become the marimba

nodes.title: Blue and white pottery
nodes.dateDetails: Porcelain came from China, but the blue cobalt glaze was likely invented in Iraq in the 8th or 9th century, and then spread back to China

nodes.title: Soda ash from plants
nodes.subtitle: aka Barilla, Sodium carbonate
nodes.dateDetails: Sodium carbonate derived from halophytic plant ash (as opposed to natron deposits). The transition from natron in glassmaking occurred "in the late 8th-early 9th century".

nodes.title: Toothbrush
nodes.dateDetails: approximate, Tang dynasty [619–907]

nodes.title: Maple syrup
nodes.dateDetails: extremely approximate; first documented by Europeans in the early 1600s, but is known to be far older; there is some archeological evidence from the Late Woodland period and the Mackinac phase (750-1000)

nodes.title: Talking drum
nodes.dateDetails: hard to find good sources for dating, but some suggest the 7th century in the Ghana Empire

nodes.title: Whale oil
nodes.dateDetails: There is a document dating from 670 about the purchase of whale oil and blubber from Bayonne in the Basque country, by the Jumièges Abbey in Normandy; this may be the oldest evidence of whale oil being sold for illumination purposes.

nodes.title: Carruca
nodes.subtitle: aka Heavy plough
nodes.dateDetails: Could be earlier (568) based on linguistic evidence from the Slavic area

nodes.title: Positional decimal numerals and true zero
nodes.subtitle: Hindu-Arabic numerals
nodes.dateDetails: date of the Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta, the first book to clearly explain the role of the zero, completing earlier developments that made the Hindu-Arabic numerals a complete positional decimal system

nodes.title: Carvel boat building
nodes.dateDetails: date of the Yassi Ada shipwreck; carvel building probably began to appear around 500

nodes.title: Tide mill
nodes.dateDetails: "Dendrochronology has dated a tide mill on [Mahee Island] to the year 619, making this the oldest excavated tide mill anywhere in the world."

nodes.title: Pipe organ
nodes.dateDetails: "By the 6th or 7th century AD, bellows were used to supply Byzantine organs with wind"

nodes.title: Timpani
nodes.subtitle: aka Naker, Naqareh, Kettledrum
nodes.dateDetails: or older

nodes.title: Ammonium chloride
nodes.subtitle: aka Sal ammoniac
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest mention of ammonium chloride was in 554 in China. At that time, ammonium chloride came from two sources: (1) the vents of underground coal fires in Central Asia, specifically, in the Tian Shan mountains (which extend from Xinjiang province of northwestern China through Kyrgyzstan) as well as in the Alay (or Alai) mountains of southwestern Kyrgyzstan, and (2) the fumaroles of the volcano Mount Taftan in southeastern Iran"

nodes.title: Kite
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest clear evidence of paper kites being flown in China, but silk kites may have been invented much earlier (4th century BC).

nodes.title: Wheelchair
nodes.dateDetails: People in China and Greece used wheeled furniture to move both objects and disabled people from the 6th-5th centuries BC, but "a distinction between the two functions was not made for another several hundred years, until around AD 525, when images of wheeled chairs made specifically to carry people began to occur in Chinese art."

nodes.title: Candle clock
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest reference to their use to occurs in a Chinese poem by You Jiangu (AD 520)."

nodes.title: Pointed arch bridge
nodes.subtitle: Karamagara Bridge
nodes.details: Image shows the Getar bridge in Armenia

nodes.title: Nailed horseshoe
nodes.dateDetails: from a 5th century tomb in Belgium
nodes.details: oldest horseshoes with possible nails are known from an Etruscan tomb dated 4th century BC, but there are no other finds/references until the 5th century AD

nodes.title: Quill
nodes.dateDetails: The quill begins replacing the reed pen in Europe in the 6th century

nodes.title: Ultramarine
nodes.dateDetails: Lapis-lazuli was mined "and traded to Mesopotamia and ancient Egypt as early as the third millennium BCE", but used as a pigment starting around the 5th-7th centuries in Buddhist and Zoroastrian caves.

nodes.title: Palm-leaf manuscript
nodes.dateDetails: 5th century

nodes.title: Paddle wheel boat
nodes.dateDetails: approximate, 4th or 5th century

nodes.title: Dichroic glass
nodes.subtitle: Lycurgus cup
nodes.dateDetails: 4th century
nodes.details: data for the Lycurgus cup, the earliest known item made of dichroic glass

nodes.title: Metallic nib
nodes.dateDetails: Oldest one found from Roman Britain

nodes.title: Onager
nodes.dateDetails: Date of first mention, by Ammianus Marcellinus
nodes.details: This is the design most commonly associated with catapults, which was a generic word for artillery in Greco-Roman times

nodes.title: Crystallized sugar
nodes.dateDetails: approximate

nodes.title: Fishing reel
nodes.dateDetails: approximate, 3rd or 4th century

nodes.title: Water turbine
nodes.dateDetails: late 3rd or early 4th century AD

nodes.title: Rock-cut stepwell
nodes.dateDetails: The first rock-cut stepwells in India date from 200–400 AD.

nodes.title: Crank-slider mechanism
nodes.subtitle: Hierapolis sawmill
nodes.dateDetails: second half of the 3rd century AD

nodes.title: Lashed-lug boat building
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest recovered archeological evidence, but may be much older

nodes.title: South-pointing chariot
nodes.dateDetails: attributed to Ma Jun,

nodes.title: Woodblock printing
nodes.dateDetails: or earlier

nodes.title: Crank
nodes.dateDetails: Difficult to date. Seems to have possibly been invented much earlier than 200 AD (150 BCE, Antikythera mechanism; or during the Western Han dynasty (202 BC – 9 AD) in China.

nodes.title: Platinum
nodes.dateDetails: "Archeologists usually associate the tradition of platinum-working in South America with the La Tolita Culture (c. 600 BCE – 200 CE), but precise dates and location are difficult"

nodes.title: Lateen and settee sail
nodes.dateDetails: The lateen originated in the Mediterranean as early as the 2nd century AD, during Roman times, and became common there by the 5th century.

nodes.title: Pipa
nodes.dateDetails: Han dynasty

nodes.title: Glass mirror
nodes.dateDetails: Pliny mentioned that artisans and Sidon were making glass mirrors in the 1st century, but the first archeological evidence of glass mirrors comes from the 3rd century

nodes.title: Alembic
nodes.dateDetails: approximate, based on possible dates for Mary the Jewess (1st-3rd centuries) and Cleopatra the Alchemist (c. 300)

nodes.title: Ptolemaic geocentrism
nodes.dateDetails: Plausible date of Ptolemy's Almagest

nodes.title: Tanja sail
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest pictorial evidence is the Borobudur ship, carved circa the 8th-9th centuries, but descriptions suggest it was used for k'un-lun po ships in the early 1st millennium.

nodes.title: Quadrant
nodes.dateDetails: There are mentions of quadrants being used to measure a solar eclipse during Rigvedic times in India (1500-1000 BC), but more historically verifiable accounts start with Ptolemy.

nodes.title: K'un-lun po
nodes.dateDetails: Mentioned by Ptolemy in 150 AD, and Chinese sources in the 3rd century

nodes.title: Wheelbarrow
nodes.dateDetails: Probably around 100 AD; first archeological evidence on murals from 118 AD. Possibly described around 407 BC in Greece, but no archeological evidence so far. The consensus still that it spread from China to the rest of the world.

nodes.title: Papermaking
nodes.details: Cai Lun is generally credited as the inventor of paper, but there is archeological evidence that paper predates him by centuries. He likely came up with method improvements that turned paper into an industry.

nodes.title: Pivoted scissors
nodes.details: Modern scissor design

nodes.title: Odometer (China)
nodes.dateDetails: "possibly by the prolific inventor and early scientist Zhang Heng (78 AD – 139 AD)"

nodes.title: Fire piston
nodes.dateDetails: "The antiquity of the fire piston in Southeast Asia is unknown, but it definitely pre-dated the Austronesian colonization of Madagascar (c. 100-500 AD)."

nodes.title: Sternpost-mounted rudder
nodes.dateDetails: 1st century, Eastern Han dynasty

nodes.title: Latitude and longitude
nodes.dateDetails: approximate, based on Marinus of Tyre's lifetime. Influenced Ptolemy's Geography

nodes.title: Mould-board plough
nodes.dateDetails: Han dynasty "in the 1st and 2nd century"

nodes.title: Divinatory compass
nodes.dateDetails: Referenced in the Luheng, a book written c. 70-80

nodes.title: Hand plane
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest examples known from Pompeii. Basic design may be much more ancient

nodes.title: Birch bark manuscript
nodes.dateDetails: The oldest birch bark scrolls from Gandhara are dated to the first half of the 1st century

nodes.title: Hydraulic mining
nodes.subtitle: Hushing, Ruina montium
nodes.dateDetails: At Las Médulas, "large-scale production did not begin until the second half of the 1st century AD."

nodes.title: Glass window
nodes.dateDetails: Seneca, who died in 64, mentioned glass windows as a recent invention; physical evidence at Pompeii in 79

nodes.title: Fluked anchor
nodes.dateDetails: Intact anchors with the familiar modern design and iron parts were found on the Nemi ships, from the reign of Caligula (37-41 AD); may be older, and descend from less advanced designs

nodes.title: Soap
nodes.dateDetails: "Knowledge of how to produce true soap emerged at some point between early mentions of proto-soaps and the first century AD."
nodes.details: Attributed to "the Gauls" by Pliny

nodes.title: Snow goggles
nodes.dateDetails: Most common date on various websites seems to be "2000 years ago"

nodes.title: Speculum metal mirror
nodes.subtitle: aka High-tin bronze
nodes.dateDetails: "The knowledge of making very hard white high luster metal out of bronze-type high-tin alloys may date back more than 2000 years in China,[3] although it could also be an invention of western civilizations.[4] Remarks in Pliny the Elder may refer to it."

nodes.title: Combination lock
nodes.dateDetails: The oldest combination lock known is from Athens from the early imperial Roman era, 27 BC to 138 AD: "Am wahrscheinlichsten ist, wie F. Willemsen mitteilt, eine Entstehung in augusteischer oder hadrianischer Zeit"

nodes.title: Lapis specularis window
nodes.dateDetails: approximate

nodes.title: Reverse overshot water wheel
nodes.dateDetails: or before; -25 is the description by Vitruvius

nodes.title: Odometer (Western)
nodes.dateDetails: or earlier: "An odometer for measuring distance was first described by Vitruvius around 27 and 23 BC, during the First Punic War, although the actual inventor may have been Archimedes of Syracuse (c. 287 BC – c. 212 BC)."

nodes.title: Treadwheel crane
nodes.dateDetails: Vitruvius's De architectura (written circa 30-20 BCE) contains a description of the Polyspaston crane. There are surviving reliefs of treadwheel cranes during the 1st century AD.

nodes.title: Maya numerals and zero
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest long count date (on Stela 2 at Chiappa de Corzo, Chiapas) is from 36 BC."

nodes.title: Trip hammer
nodes.details: "Although Chinese historians assert that its origins may span as far back as the Zhou Dynasty (1050 BC–221 BC), the British sinologist Joseph Needham regards the earliest texts to describe the device are the Jijiupian dictionary of 40 BC" and other texts from 15 BC and 20 AD

nodes.title: Arch dam
nodes.dateDetails: approximate (Glanum Dam in the 1st century BC, but after the conquest of southern Gaul by Julius Caesar)

nodes.title: Segmental arched bridge
nodes.subtitle: Ponte San Lorenzo
nodes.details: Image shows the Ponte Molino, another Roman-era segmental arch bridge in Padua

nodes.title: Codex
nodes.dateDetails: approximate, based on Julius Caesar's lifetime. May be older — possible evidence from Ptolemaic Egypt

nodes.title: Armillary sphere (China)
nodes.dateDetails: May date from the 4th century BC, but some historians doubt this. The armillary sphere reached its full development "by 52 BC, with the astronomer Geng Shouchang's (耿壽昌) addition of a permanently fixed equatorial ring."

nodes.title: Gristmill
nodes.subtitle: aka Grain mill, Flour mill
nodes.dateDetails: Or before: The Greek geographer Strabo reported in his Geography that a water-powered grain-mill existed near the palace of king Mithradates VI Eupator at Cabira, Asia Minor, before 71 BC."

nodes.title: Hypocaust
nodes.dateDetails: Attributed to Sergius Orata by Vitruvius; probably older (e.g. dating to the Temple of Ephesus in 350 BC or the Stabian Baths in Pompeii in 125 BC)

nodes.title: Oyster farming
nodes.dateDetails: Sometimes credited to Sergius Orata circa 95 BC

nodes.title: Toe stirrup
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest foot support was a toe loop that held the big toe and was used in India late in the second century BC, though it may have appeared as early as 500 BC."

nodes.title: Barbat
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest image of the barbat dates back to the 1st century BC from ancient northern Bactria."

nodes.title: War wagon
nodes.dateDetails: First documented use in battle, but may be far older

nodes.title: Tea
nodes.dateDetails: "In 2016, the discovery of the earliest known physical evidence of tea from the mausoleum of Emperor Jing of Han (d. 141 BCE) in Xi'an was announced, indicating that tea from the genus Camellia was drunk by Han dynasty (202 BCE–220 CE) emperors as early as the 2nd century BCE." Tea drinking  may be much older, but there is no clear evidence.

nodes.title: True arch bridge
nodes.subtitle: Pons Aemilius
nodes.dateDetails: Arches of the Pons Aemilius built in 142 BC. There may be older examples

nodes.title: Analog computer
nodes.subtitle: Antikythera mechanism
nodes.details: Antikythera mechanism possibly from Rhodes, possibly involved Hipparchus.

nodes.title: Roman concrete
nodes.dateDetails: "Roman concrete was in widespread use from about 150 BC; some scholars believe it was developed a century before that."

nodes.title: Globe
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest known example, by Crates of Mallus, but the first globes probably date from when the sphericity of the Earth was established

nodes.title: Paper
nodes.dateDetails: The oldest known paper fragment is a map from Fangmatan, Gansu, China, dated to 179–141 BCE

nodes.title: Cupronickel
nodes.subtitle: aka Copper–nickel alloy, Paktong
nodes.dateDetails: Coins are known from Bactria from 180 or 170 BC. Knowledge of cupronickel may date from the 3rd century BC in Yunnan, China

nodes.title: Astrolabe
nodes.dateDetails: between 220 and 150 BC

nodes.title: Parchment
nodes.details: Traditionally said to have been invented in Pergamon as a response to the cessation of papyrus imports from Egypt. Real date probably more than 1000 years earlier.

nodes.title: Finery forge
nodes.dateDetails: "A finery forge was used to refine wrought iron at least by the 3rd century BC in ancient China, based on the earliest archaeological specimens of cast and pig iron fined into wrought iron and steel found at the early Han dynasty (202 BC – 220 AD) site at Tieshengguo. Pigott speculates that the finery forge existed in the previous Warring States period (403–221 BC), because of the wrought iron items from China dating to that period and there was no documented evidence of the bloomery ever being used in China."

nodes.title: Celestial globe
nodes.dateDetails: Oldest extant depiction is the Farnese Atlas, a statue dated 150 CE, but the invention was probably several centuries earlier and the statue is probably a copy of a Hellenistic era one

nodes.title: Drawloom
nodes.dateDetails: 200 BC is the date supported by the best archaeological evidence (Laoguanshan). 400 BC is plausible, based on inferring from surviving textiles that a pattern loom existed during the Warring States period.

nodes.title: Padlock
nodes.dateDetails: "Padlocks have been used in Europe since the middle La Tène period, subsequently spreading to the Roman world and the Przeworsk and Chernyakhov cultures." Middle La Tène is approximately 300 to 100 BC

nodes.title: Elevator
nodes.dateDetails: The earliest reference to an elevator is by Vitruvius, who attributes it to Archimedes

nodes.title: Water organ
nodes.subtitle: aka Hydraulis
nodes.details: Ctesibius' most commonly known invention today is a pipe organ (hydraulis), a predecessor of the modern church organ.

nodes.title: Gimbal
nodes.dateDetails: approximate, based on Philo's lifetime
nodes.details: Philo described it and the invention is sometimes attributed to him

nodes.title: Suction pump
nodes.dateDetails: Very approximate. Dates from antiquity; examples have been found in Pompeii. Date chosen to fit with Ctesibius, who wrote a treaty about pumps.

nodes.title: Saqiyah
nodes.subtitle: aka Sakia, Tympanum
nodes.dateDetails: 3rd century BC, about a century after norias

nodes.title: Cheque
nodes.subtitle: Adesha
nodes.dateDetails: late 4th century BC

nodes.title: Saltpeter
nodes.subtitle: aka Potassium nitrate
nodes.dateDetails: "Saltpeter finds mention in Kautilya's Arthashastra (compiled 300BC - 300AD), which mentions using its poisonous smoke as a weapon of war, although its use for propulsion did not appear until medieval times."

nodes.title: Chain mail
nodes.subtitle: aka Hauberk
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest examples of surviving mail were found in the Carpathian Basin at a burial in Horný Jatov, Slovakia dated in the 3rd century BC, and in a chieftain's burial located in Ciumești, Romania. Its invention is commonly credited to the Celts, but there are examples of Etruscan pattern mail dating from at least the 4th century BC."

nodes.title: White lead
nodes.dateDetails: Probably used earlier, but written about by Theophrastus c. 300 BC.

nodes.title: Verdigris
nodes.dateDetails: Written about by Theophrastus c. 300 BC.

nodes.title: Keel
nodes.subtitle: Kyrenia ship
nodes.dateDetails: Launch date of the Kyrenia, the oldest recovered ship with a keel

nodes.title: Caltrop
nodes.dateDetails: First documented at the Battle of Gaugamela (331 BC)

nodes.title: Diving bell
nodes.dateDetails: 4th century; described by Aristotle

nodes.title: Pike
nodes.subtitle: Sarissa
nodes.dateDetails: approximate; began being used tactically during the reign of Philip II of Macedon (359-336)

nodes.title: Noria and water wheel
nodes.dateDetails: 4th century BC

nodes.title: Animal-driven rotary mill
nodes.dateDetails: Evidence in a shipwreck dated 375 to 350 BC
nodes.details: possibly in Carthaginian Sardinia

nodes.title: Ballista
nodes.dateDetails: The earliest form of the ballista is thought to have been developed for Dionysius of Syracuse, c. 400 BC.

nodes.title: Camera obscura
nodes.dateDetails: first written description, attributed to Mozi (470-391 BC)

nodes.title: Embroidery
nodes.dateDetails: "Works in China have been dated to the Warring States period (5th–3rd century BC)."

nodes.title: Muslin
nodes.dateDetails: "Muslin finds mention in Megasthenes’ writings, a Greek envoy to the court of Chandragupta Maurya in the 4th century BC"

nodes.title: Method of exhaustion
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate. Attributed to Antiphon, who died in 411 BC

nodes.title: Crossbow (Greece)
nodes.subtitle: Gastraphetes
nodes.dateDetails: "The terminus ante quem may be more precisely defined as being before 421 BC, since another Greek author, Biton (fl. 2nd century BC), whose reliability has been positively reevaluated by recent scholarship, credits two advanced forms of the gastraphetes to a certain Zopyros."
nodes.details: It's possible that crossbows spread from China to Europe, but there are differences in mechanism

nodes.title: Alum
nodes.dateDetails: approximate, based on Herodotus who mentioned it first in his Histories

nodes.title: Scythed chariot
nodes.dateDetails: reign of Ajatashatru: c. 492 to 460 BCE or early 5th century BCE

nodes.title: Lighthouse
nodes.dateDetails: "Themistocles had earlier established a lighthouse at the harbour of Piraeus connected to Athens in the 5th century BC, essentially a small stone column with a fire beacon"

nodes.title: Rotary quern
nodes.details: possibly in Celtiberian Spain, possibly in Carthage

nodes.title: Sphericity of the Earth
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest documented mention of the concept dates from around the 5th century BC, when it appears in the writings of Greek philosophers." May be older

nodes.title: Three-field crop rotation
nodes.dateDetails: Eastern Zhou period, 771-256 BCE. Invented independently during the European Middle Ages.

nodes.title: Muscle cuirass
nodes.dateDetails: "It first appears in late Archaic Greece and became widespread throughout the 5th and 4th centuries BC."

nodes.title: Cork
nodes.dateDetails: Often described as dating from 3000 BC in online source, but not confirmed by archeology or scholarly sources. Use starting in the 5th century is better attested

nodes.title: Cataract surgery
nodes.subtitle: Couching
nodes.details: speculatively, may be mentioned in Mesopotamian and Egyptian sources

nodes.title: Wagonway
nodes.subtitle: Diolkos
nodes.details: the Diolkos, built to move ships across the isthmus of Corinth

nodes.title: Caliper
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest calipers found in a shipwreck in Italy dated to c. 600 BC

nodes.title: Placeholder zero
nodes.dateDetails: "In a tablet unearthed at Kish (dating to as early as 700 BC), the scribe Bêl-bân-aplu used three hooks as a placeholder in the same Babylonian system."

nodes.title: Saddle
nodes.details: The earliest known saddle-like equipment were fringed cloths or pads used by Assyrian cavalry around 700 BC.

nodes.title: Candle
nodes.dateDetails: "It is often believed that the use of wicked candles developed in Italy in the Etruscan period; a picture of a candlestick exists in an Etruscan tomb at Orvieto, and the earliest Etruscan candlestick may date from the 7th century BC"

nodes.title: Dentures
nodes.dateDetails: "As early as the 7th century BC, Etruscans in northern Italy made partial dentures out of human or other animal teeth fastened together with gold bands."

nodes.title: Trireme
nodes.dateDetails: Pliny the Elder says that Thucydides mentioned Ameinocles as the inventor of the trireme. This is not true, but Ameinocles was building triremes in Greece in 704 BC.

nodes.title: Magnifying glass
nodes.subtitle: Nimrud lens
nodes.details: speculative: the Nimrud lens may have been a magnifying glass, or just a decorative item. There are clear references to magnifying glasses in Aristophanes (5th century BC)

nodes.title: Screw
nodes.dateDetails: "It first appeared in Mesopotamia during the Neo-Assyrian period (911-609) BC"

nodes.title: Lamellar armour
nodes.dateDetails: Neo-Assyrian Empire era, 911 BC–609 BC

nodes.title: Bireme
nodes.dateDetails: 8th century BC

nodes.title: Kithara
nodes.dateDetails: Mentioned by Homer

nodes.title: Sponge
nodes.dateDetails: Mentioned by Homer in the Odyssey

nodes.title: Han purple and Han blue
nodes.dateDetails: Western Zhou period (1045–771 BC); beads from 800 BC are mentioned in Berke (2007)

nodes.title: Writing (Mesoamerica)
nodes.subtitle: Olmec script
nodes.details: Controversial

nodes.title: Macuahuitl
nodes.dateDetails: "Clubs or shafts with obsidian blades lining them are clearly pretty ancient: The San Lorenzo monuments as I said would probably date back to 1400-900BC"

nodes.title: Distillation
nodes.dateDetails: date of Akkadian tablets describing perfumery operations

nodes.title: Bloomery
nodes.dateDetails: conventional start date of the Iron Age, when smelted iron became widespread

nodes.title: Concrete
nodes.dateDetails: Concrete floors were found in the royal palace of Tiryns, Greece, which dates roughly to 1400-1200 BC.

nodes.title: Corbel arch bridge
nodes.subtitle: Arkadiko Bridge
nodes.dateDetails: Late Helladic period (III) (ca. 1300–1190 BC)

nodes.title: Vitreous enamel
nodes.subtitle: aka Porcelain enamel
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest undisputed objects known to use enamel are a group of Mycenaean rings from Cyprus, dated to the 13th century BC"

nodes.title: Phoenician joint
nodes.subtitle: Uluburun ship
nodes.dateDetails: date of the Uluburun ship ± 50 years

nodes.title: Pigeon post
nodes.dateDetails: "Homing pigeons were potentially being used for pigeon post in Ancient Egypt by 1350 BCE."

nodes.title: Scale armour
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest representation is the tomb of Kenamon, who lived in Egypt in the reign of Amenhotep II (1436–1411 BC)."

nodes.title: Pewter
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest known object made of pewter, but pewter was probably used much earlier

nodes.title: Spring scissors
nodes.dateDetails: Unclear; many online sources say 1500 BC in Egypt but, but difficult to know what to trust. Some also say 2000 BC in Mesopotamia.

nodes.title: Domestication of coconuts
nodes.dateDetails: sometime during the Austronesian expansion (3000 to 1500 BCE)

nodes.title: Smalt
nodes.subtitle: aka Cobalt glass
nodes.details: Glass colored with cobalt oxide is called "smalt" when ground into a pigment

nodes.title: Water clock
nodes.subtitle: aka Clepsydra
nodes.details: May need to split this entry into various regional water clocks. This entry currently reflects Egyptian clocks.

nodes.title: Rubber
nodes.dateDetails: or earlier
nodes.details: first evidence is in Mesoamerican game balls

nodes.title: Star chart
nodes.subtitle: Nebra sky disc
nodes.dateDetails: earlier findings (up to 33,000 BC) have been interpreted as representing stars, but the Nebra sky disc is the earliest concrete depiction of the night sky. Star charts also appear in the 1500s BC in Egypt

nodes.title: Planetary theory
nodes.subtitle: Venus tablet of Ammisaduqa
nodes.dateDetails: "The Babylonians were the first civilization known to possess a functional theory of the planets". The earliest known text must have been compiled during the reign of Ammisaduqa, in the mid-17th century BCE

nodes.title: Pythagorean theorem
nodes.dateDetails: Clear knowledge of Pythagorean triplets is found in Egyptian and Babylonian documents from 1800 BCE, long before Pythagoras was born

nodes.title: Depth sounding
nodes.dateDetails: ancient, described in the New Testament and found in Greco-Roman archeology

nodes.title: Zither
nodes.subtitle: Qanun
nodes.dateDetails: "The qanun traces one of its origins to a stringed Assyrian instrument from the Old Assyrian Empire, specifically from the nineteenth century BC in Mesopotamia."

nodes.title: Abjad
nodes.subtitle: Proto-Sinaitic script
nodes.dateDetails: The principal debate is between an early date, around 1850 BC, and a late date, around 1550 BC

nodes.title: Pulley
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest evidence of pulleys dates back to Ancient Egypt in the Twelfth Dynasty (1991–1802 BC) and Mesopotamia in the early 2nd millennium BC."

nodes.title: Catamaran
nodes.dateDetails: Uncertain; plausibly around the same time as the crab claw sail

nodes.title: Aqueduct
nodes.dateDetails: Developed by many ancient civilizations, probably starting with the Minoans around 2000 BCE

nodes.title: Domestication of citrus fruit
nodes.subtitle: Pomelo and mandarin orange
nodes.dateDetails: Most studies state "4000 years ago"

nodes.title: Composite bow
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate. Probably invented by North Asian or Central Asian steppe nomads, but the archeological record is patchy. The oldest composite bows are from Egypt or China in the 2nd millennium BC, but they may be several thousands of years older.

nodes.title: Noodles
nodes.subtitle: aka Pasta
nodes.dateDetails: The earliest archeological evidence for noodles is from  the Lajia site, made by the Qijia culture, 4000 years ago.

nodes.title: Galley
nodes.dateDetails: Evidence from the reign of pharaoh Pepi I (2332–2283 BC)

nodes.title: Copper pipes
nodes.details: Pyramid of Sahure in Egypt

nodes.title: Iron smelting and wrought iron
nodes.dateDetails: "One of the earliest smelted iron artifacts, a dagger with an iron blade found in a Hattic tomb in Anatolia, dated from 2500 BC."

nodes.title: Incense
nodes.dateDetails: oldest known incense burner dates from the 5th dynasty of Egypt (which starts approximately in 2500 BCE) but burning incense may be much older

nodes.title: Electrum
nodes.dateDetails: mentioned during the reign of Pharaoh Sahure, reigned uncertain dates during the 25th century BC

nodes.title: Cloisonné
nodes.dateDetails: 3rd millennium BC

nodes.title: Butter
nodes.dateDetails: First clear evidence, from a Sumerian tablet, at which point butter-making was an intentional product. But butter was probably made far earlier.

nodes.title: Solar calendar
nodes.subtitle: Egyptian calendar
nodes.dateDetails: "The civil calendar was established at some early date in or before the Old Kingdom, with probable evidence of its use early in the reign of Shepseskaf (c. 2510 BC, Dynasty IV) and certain attestation during the reign of Neferirkare (mid-25th century BC, Dynasty V)."

nodes.title: Reed pen
nodes.dateDetails: Oldest reed pens found in Egypt date from 400 BCE, but they probably date to the first written papyri

nodes.title: Papyrus
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest estimate for the Diary of Merer, the oldest papyrus found. Manufacture of papyrus is probably older

nodes.title: Scroll
nodes.details: The oldest known scroll is the Diary of Merer, which can be dated to c. 2568 BCE.

nodes.title: Ivory carving
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate, based on the fact that the trade of ivory products is first recorded in the Indus Valley civilization, and an ivory die dated 2600-1900 BC.

nodes.title: True arch
nodes.dateDetails: Known at least since the Third dynasty of Egypt (c. 2686 BC–c. 2613 BC)

nodes.title: Reservoir
nodes.details: Image of the Great Hafir in Sudan

nodes.title: Domestication of llamas and alpacas
nodes.dateDetails: 6000-7000 years ago. "While their wild relatives, the guanaco and the vicuña, have been determined to have diverged from their common ancestor two to three million years ago (Stanley et al., 1994; Wheeler, 1995; Fan et al., 2020), the domestic llama and alpaca are assumed to have been domesticated 6,000 to 7,000 years ago (Wheeler, 1995)"

nodes.title: Corbel arch
nodes.dateDetails: Approximate, varies across cultures. Clear examples around 2600 BC in Egypt, where corbel arches were preferred to true arches despite the latter being known around that time.

nodes.title: Sewn boat building
nodes.subtitle: Abydos boats
nodes.details: Image shows the Khufu ship, one of the earliest sewn boats (2500 BCE)

nodes.title: Shield
nodes.dateDetails: Believed to have been produced in the Late Neolithic. The oldest archeological evidence of a shield seems to be the from Globular Amphora culture (3400-2800 BC). 3100 BC is the middle of this range.

nodes.title: Iron
nodes.subtitle: Meteoric iron
nodes.dateDetails: Sources vary, up to 5000 BC for meteoric iron beads in Egypt or Western Asia; beads from Gerzeh, Egypt dated to 3200 BC are commonly cited

nodes.title: Harp
nodes.subtitle: Arched harp
nodes.dateDetails: The two most ancient images of arched harps are dated 3300-3100 BC, in Israel (Megiddo) and Iran (Chogha Mish)

nodes.title: Egyptian blue
nodes.details: First synthetic pigment

nodes.title: Opium poppy cultivation
nodes.dateDetails: "The first known cultivation of opium poppies was in Mesopotamia, approximately 3400 BCE, by Sumerians, who called the plant hul gil, the "joy plant"."

nodes.title: Shoe
nodes.dateDetails: "The world's oldest leather shoe, made from a single piece of cowhide laced with a leather cord along seams at the front and back, was found in the Areni-1 cave complex in Armenia in 2008 and is believed to date to 3500 BC."

nodes.title: Plumbing
nodes.subtitle: Clay sewer pipes from the Temple of Bel at Nippur

nodes.title: Fast potter's wheel
nodes.details: various possible origins: Sumer, southeast Europe, China, Indus Valley civilizaton

nodes.title: Wheel
nodes.details: It is not known whether Chinese, Indians, Europeans and even Sumerians invented the wheel independently or not.

nodes.title: Straight razor
nodes.dateDetails: "Excavations in Egypt have unearthed solid gold and copper razors in tombs dating back to the 4th millennium BC."

nodes.title: Natron
nodes.dateDetails: "Natron deposits, the best known of which being those at Wadi Natrun in Egypt, have been used as the flux in the production of vitreous materials from the early 4th millennium BC onwards." (Shortland et al. 2006)

nodes.title: Egyptian faience
nodes.details: Unrelated to the other material called "faience"

nodes.title: Kermes dye
nodes.subtitle: aka Crimson
nodes.dateDetails: late Neolithic Mediterranean region, evidence from the Adaouste cave in southern France

nodes.title: Graphite
nodes.dateDetails: Evidence of graphite-painted pottery during phase III of the Boian culture in the Balkans (4100-4000 BC)

nodes.title: Paddy field
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest paddy field found dates to 4330 BC, based on carbon dating of grains of rice and soil organic matter found at the Chaodun site in Kunshan."

nodes.title: Gold
nodes.dateDetails: Middle of the dating range (4600-4200 BC) of the Varna Necropolis in Bulgaria, where the oldest known gold artifacts were found. Small amounts of gold have been found at much older Paleolithic sites.

nodes.title: Fired bricks
nodes.details: invented in both China and Mesopotamia, details unclear

nodes.title: Lost-wax casting
nodes.countryHistorical: Prehistoric Europe, Prehistoric Middle East, Indus Valley civilization
nodes.formattedLocation: Prehistoric Europe; Prehistoric Middle East; Indus Valley civilization
nodes.details: oldest lost wax objects in Bulgaria; other early examples in Israel and Indus Valley

nodes.title: Tournette
nodes.subtitle: aka Slow potter's wheel
nodes.details: aka slow wheel, a precursor to the fast potter's wheel

nodes.title: Bow drill
nodes.dateDetails: "Bow drills with green jasper bits were used in Mehrgarh between the 4th and 5th millennium BC"

nodes.title: Sling
nodes.dateDetails: Oldest surviving slings date from 2500 BC in Peru, but stones suspected to have been for slings were found in the Wadi Rabah culture, from the mid-5th millennium in the Levant.
nodes.details: Likely an independent invention by many cultures

nodes.title: Drum
nodes.subtitle: Alligator drum
nodes.details: First artificial drums found date from Neolithic China

nodes.title: Cheese
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest evidence of cheesemaking in archeological record. Probably older, perhaps 8,000 BC after the domestication of sheep.

nodes.title: Mummification
nodes.dateDetails: "tests on a 5,600-year-old mummy in Turin revealed that it had been deliberately mummified using linen wrappings and embalming oils made from conifer resin and aromatic plant extracts"

nodes.title: Ard plough
nodes.subtitle: aka Scratch plough, Ard
nodes.dateDetails: mid-Neolithic / 6th millennium BC

nodes.title: Olive oil
nodes.dateDetails: "Archaeological evidence in Galilee shows that by 6000 BC olives were being turned into olive oil"

nodes.title: Whaling
nodes.dateDetails: estimated date of the Bangudae petroglyphs, the earliest known depiction of whaling, in Korea

nodes.title: Warp-weighted loom
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest evidence of warp-weighted looms comes from sites belonging to the Starčevo culture in modern Serbia and Hungary from late Neolithic sites in Switzerland."

nodes.title: Construction mortar
nodes.dateDetails: "According to Roman Ghirshman, the first evidence of humans using a form of mortar was at the Mehrgarh of Baluchistan in what is today Pakistan, built of sun-dried bricks in 6500 BC"

nodes.title: Nålebinding
nodes.dateDetails: "The oldest known textile fragment of nålebinding, dating from c. 6500 BCE, was found in Nahal Hemar Cave, in the Judean Desert."

nodes.title: Lunar and lunisolar calendars
nodes.dateDetails: "A lunisolar calendar was found at Warren Field in Scotland and has been dated to c. 8000 BC". But may be much older.

nodes.title: Rammed earth
nodes.dateDetails: "Evidence of ancient use of rammed earth has been found in Neolithic archaeological sites such as those of the Fertile Crescent, dating to the 9th–7th millennium BC"

nodes.title: Boat
nodes.subtitle: Dugout canoe
nodes.dateDetails: date of the oldest known boat, the Pesse canoe

nodes.title: Stairs
nodes.dateDetails: There is a staircase at Göbekli Tepe Layer II, dated to the 9th millennium BC

nodes.title: Stonemasonry
nodes.dateDetails: Layer III of Göbekli Tepe

nodes.title: Domestication of pigs (Near East)
nodes.dateDetails: Remains of pigs have been dated to earlier than 11,400 years ago in Cyprus. Those animals must have been introduced from the mainland, which suggests domestication in the adjacent mainland by then

nodes.title: Hoe
nodes.dateDetails: approximate, based on beginnings of agriculture

nodes.title: Alcohol fermentation
nodes.dateDetails: "The oldest verifiable brewery has been found in a prehistoric burial site in a cave near Haifa in modern-day Israel. Researchers have found residue of 13,000-year-old beer that they think might have been used for ritual feasts to honor the dead. The traces of a wheat-and-barley-based alcohol were found in stone mortars carved into the cave floor."

nodes.title: Bread
nodes.dateDetails: Made by hunter-gatherers in Jordan from wild wheat and barley: "The oldest evidence of bread-making has been found in a 14,500-year-old Natufian site in Jordan's northeastern desert."

nodes.title: Oil lamp
nodes.dateDetails: "Hundreds of these lamps (hollow worked stones) have been found in the Lascaux caves in modern-day France, dating to about 15,000 years ago."

nodes.title: Fish hook
nodes.dateDetails: oldest fish hooks (made from sea snail shells) were discovered in Sakitari Cave in Okinawa Island

nodes.title: Fishing net
nodes.dateDetails: First evidence of fishing nets, from Korea

nodes.title: Charcoal
nodes.dateDetails: evidence of charcoal being used for drawing; use for burning may be much older
nodes.details: first use of pure carbon

nodes.title: Sewing needle
nodes.dateDetails: From Denisova Cave, approximately 40,000 BP

nodes.title: Tally stick
nodes.dateDetails: Date of the Lebombo bone, oldest possible tally stick, from Eswatini. Later discoveries date to -28000 (wolf bone in Czechia) and -20000 to -18000 (Ishango bone in Congo)

nodes.title: Rope and twine
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest possible evidence, attributed to Neanderthals

nodes.title: Boomerang
nodes.details: Specifically returning boomerangs. Also independently invented elsewhere (North America, Egypt, Europe)

nodes.title: Raft
nodes.dateDetails: Earliest estimated date of settlement of Australia, for which rafts would have been necessary

nodes.title: Stitching awl
nodes.dateDetails: "The earliest bone awls are found in southern Africa, at Blombos Cave approximately 73,000 to 70,000 years ago"
